initiative: The Trafalgar squeeze of global liquidity
description: by Vincent Bignon, Benoit Mojon, Miguel Ortiz Serrano The severity of financial crises is exacerbated by the lack of international liquidity or the absence of a global lender of last resort. This was evident during the Long Depression (1873-1896), the Great Depression (1929-1936), and, as we show in this paper, during the 1805-1806 crisis that followed the Battle of Trafalgar. The latter took Atlantic trade routes away from Spain and cut off Europe's access to Latin American silver, the key high-powered money of the time. This silver shortage led the Banque de France to cut lending by nearly 50% within three months, deliberately tightening credit to hoard specie and restore the value of its bank notes.

initiative: Coinbase x402 powers Amazon’s AI agent payments
description: AWS embedded Coinbase's x402 payment protocol and wallet infrastructure directly into Amazon Bedrock AgentCore on May 7, enabling autonomous AI agents to transact in USDC on the Base network without human authorization. Settlement occurs in approximately 200 milliseconds at sub-cent cost, with agents never holding private keys — authentication, signing, and execution are handled through a single API call that incorporates enterprise spending controls and compliance checks. The x402 protocol, an open HTTP-native standard built on the existing "402 Payment Required" status code, has already processed over 169 million payments in its first year, with AWS and Coinbase serving as co-founding members of the x402 Foundation alongside Cloudflare.

The core regulatory problem he identified is structural: securities law was designed around discrete intermediaries such as brokers, exchanges, and clearinghouses, whereas modern blockchain protocols can bundle all of those functions into a single piece of software, creating ambiguity about which rules apply and to whom.

initiative: UK sets new rules for fund tokenization under current regulations
description: The UK's Financial Conduct Authority published policy statement PS26/7 on April 30, confirming that asset managers can deploy distributed ledger technology for fund tokenization within the existing regulatory framework, without waiting for new primary legislation. Two structural changes are central to the guidance: an optional Direct-to-Fund dealing model that enables atomic, single-step settlement by making the fund or its depositary the direct counterparty to investors, and formal recognition of on-chain transaction records as primary books and records under the industry-led Blueprint model, removing the previous requirement to maintain a parallel off-chain register.

initiative: Piero Cipollone: Digital assets, payment efficiency and monetary policy

Cipollone argued that tokenisation qualifies as a general-purpose technology requiring coordinated adoption across the financial system, and that without a public settlement anchor, tokenised markets would be forced to rely on private instruments—most likely stablecoins—at a scale that could impair monetary policy transmission, introduce run risk, and erode monetary sovereignty.

initiative: ESMA consults on a new simplified approach to updating MMF stress test parameters
description: ESMA has opened a public consultation proposing to replace the current process of annually amending Section 5 of its Money Market Fund stress testing Guidelines with a streamlined approach under which updated calibration parameters would instead be published each year on a dedicated webpage. Under the proposal, the Guidelines themselves would remain in place to govern the overarching stress testing framework and methodology, while the website would become the single authoritative source for the latest parameter calibrations. The change is designed to allow market participants to apply revised parameters immediately upon approval, rather than waiting for a formal guidelines amendment, and forms part of ESMA's broader Simplification and Burden Reduction initiative.

The disclosure is framed around a documented tactical shift by North Korea-linked actors away from on-chain exploits toward insider-style social engineering, as illustrated by the $280 million Drift incident, where attackers gained the trust of contributors and compromised their devices rather than targeting smart contract vulnerabilities.

BlackRock's IBIT and Fidelity's FBTC dominated activity, contributing $335.5 million and $184.6 million respectively, with Morgan Stanley's MSBT adding a further $12.2 million — the remaining ten funds, including Grayscale's GBTC, reported flat flows and zero outflows.

The Dallas-based firm has added more than 2,200 BTC since its January 2026 acquisition of Semler Scientific, financing accumulation in part through its SATA preferred stock instrument, which raised $225 million against more than $600 million in demand at a roughly 13% annualized yield. Strive also carries a $50.4 million allocation to Strategy-linked preferred stock, creating cross-exposure to Michael Saylor's larger treasury structure, which holds 818,334 BTC at an average cost of $75,537.

initiative: Moscow Exchange bets bigger on crypto with SOL, XRP, TRX and BNB indexes

The exchange also plans to increase the update frequency of its existing Bitcoin and Ethereum benchmarks to every 15 seconds during trading hours and weekend sessions. The indexes are not yet designated as underlying assets for tradable products, though the exchange indicated they could serve as the basis for future instruments, consistent with Russia's model of restricting crypto-linked exposure to qualified investors through regulated derivatives rather than spot markets.

Uphold disputed the NYAG's characterization, arguing it was itself deceived by Cred and that it froze Cred's platform access within hours of learning of liquidity problems in October 2020—a position the company notes is consistent with the U.S. Department of Justice treating Uphold as a victim in its criminal case against Cred executives.

initiative: Bank groups challenge Senator Thom Tillis over stablecoin proposal

The groups cited economist Andrew Nigrinis's analysis warning that broad stablecoin adoption could reduce consumer, small-business, and farm lending by one-fifth or more, a materially different picture from White House estimates suggesting only a $2.1 billion uplift in bank lending from a yield ban.

initiative: Coinbase says crypto bill deal clears Senate path
description: Senate negotiators Thom Tillis and Angela Alsobrooks reached a compromise on the stablecoin rewards provision that had been blocking progress on the CLARITY Act, the U.S. digital asset market structure bill. Under the agreed language, rewards that are economically equivalent to bank deposit interest would be prohibited, addressing banking industry concerns that yield-bearing stablecoin products could draw deposits away from traditional lenders, while activity-based user rewards tied to platform usage would remain permissible. Coinbase, which lobbied to preserve the activity-based carve-out, confirmed the deal through its Chief Policy Officer, noting that regulators will still need to define the boundary between allowed and prohibited reward structures.

initiative: BlackRock pushes OCC to rethink tokenized reserve limits
description: BlackRock filed a comment letter with the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency opposing a proposed 20% cap on tokenized reserve assets under draft rules implementing the GENIUS Act, the federal stablecoin framework enacted in July 2025. The firm argued that reserve eligibility should be determined by credit quality, maturity, and liquidity characteristics rather than whether an asset sits on a distributed ledger, and separately sought clarification that Treasury ETFs meeting safety and liquidity standards should qualify as permissible reserves.

initiative: Iran crypto giant Nobitex hit by sanctions questions: Reuters
description: Reuters reported that Nobitex, which claims 11 million users and processes roughly 70% of Iran's crypto transactions, was founded in 2018 by Ali and Mohammad Kharrazi — members of a politically connected Iranian family — using the alternative surname Aghamir. Blockchain analysis and investigator accounts cited in the Reuters report allege the platform has facilitated transactions linked to sanctioned entities including Iran's central bank and the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, effectively functioning as a node in a parallel financial system designed to circumvent Western controls.

initiative: New York secures $5M from Uphold over CredEarn promotion
description: The New York Attorney General's office reached a settlement with crypto platform Uphold requiring it to pay more than $5 million to customers who suffered losses through CredEarn, a yield-bearing product offered in partnership with Cred, LLC between 2019 and 2020. Regulators found that Uphold failed to disclose that Cred was deploying customer funds into high-risk loans, including to uncreditworthy borrowers in China, and falsely represented that comprehensive insurance covered the product — claims the AG's office determined were materially misleading under the state's Martin Act. Cred filed for bankruptcy in late 2020, leaving affected users with unrecovered digital asset losses, and the settlement amount exceeds five times the fees Uphold collected from the arrangement.

initiative: BlackRock urges OCC to drop tokenized reserve cap idea, expand eligible assets in GENIUS Act comment letter
description: The world's largest asset manager opposed a potential 20% cap on tokenized reserve assets, a constraint that would limit products like its BUIDL fund.

initiative: SEC schedules CLARITY Act roundtable in May as Senate markup targets week of May 11
description: The SEC convened a roundtable on the CLARITY Act in May 2026, bringing together officials from the SEC and CFTC alongside crypto industry representatives to work through the core jurisdictional question of which digital assets fall under each regulator — a framework the bill would codify into permanent federal statute, building on the agencies' March 2026 joint taxonomy that classified 16 digital assets as commodities. The event was timed to run alongside the Senate Banking Committee's targeted markup the week of May 11, the first legislative action on the bill since Congress returned from recess and a compressed window given the May 21 Memorial Day deadline.

description: Mandated under the European Market Infrastructure Regulation (EMIR), the stress test aims to assess the resilience of CCPs to adverse market developments and identify any potential shortcomings. The CCP stress test framework drafted by ESMA for the purpose of this exercise is supported by an adverse market scenario provided by the European Systemic Risk Board (ESRB). initiative: ESMA launches a call for evidence on the structure of European equity markets
description: ESMA has published a call for evidence examining how trading activity in European equity markets evolved between 2022 and 2025, drawing on MiFIR transaction reporting data. While overall addressable liquidity and on-book trading have remained broadly stable, the analysis identifies a notable shift away from lit continuous trading toward closing auctions, frequent batch auctions, and systematic internaliser activity. The regulator is seeking stakeholder input on the concept of addressable liquidity, its treatment under RTS 1, and potential adjustments to post-trade transparency flagging, with responses due by 30 June 2026.

initiative: ESMA consults on guidelines on endorsement under the ESG Ratings Regulation
description: ESMA has opened a public consultation on draft guidelines governing how EU-authorised ESG rating providers can endorse ESG ratings produced by non-EU entities, a mechanism established under the ESG Ratings Regulation to extend the reach of the EU framework to third-country ratings. The consultation paper focuses specifically on the information requirements that providers must submit when applying to endorse an external rating, aiming to promote consistent application of the endorsement regime across the market. Stakeholders, including ESG rating providers, have until 29 May 2026 to submit responses, with ESMA expecting to finalise the guidelines by the end of July 2026.

initiative: Joint Committee annual report highlights digitalisation, cyber resilience and sustainable finance as key priorities of 2025
description: The Joint Committee of the three European Supervisory Authorities — EBA, EIOPA, and ESMA — published its Annual Report for 2025, detailing cross-sectoral supervisory work conducted over the year under EIOPA's chairship. The report identifies DORA implementation, sustainable finance disclosure effectiveness, and consumer protection in digital markets as the dominant workstreams, alongside contributions to simplifying the EU regulatory framework in areas including PRIIPs and sustainable finance. The Committee also advanced work on the EU securitisation framework and the European Single Access Point, operating against a backdrop of heightened geopolitical uncertainty and rapid financial innovation.

initiative: Brazil's central bank bans stablecoin and crypto settlement in cross-border payments
description: Brazil's central bank issued Resolution No. 561 on April 30, prohibiting licensed electronic foreign exchange (eFX) providers from using stablecoins or other cryptocurrencies — including USDT, USDC, and Bitcoin — as settlement instruments in cross-border payment flows, with the rule taking effect October 1, 2026. The measure directly disrupts the operational model of firms such as Nomad and Braza Bank, which had embedded stablecoin rails — including Ripple's network and XRP Ledger-issued real-backed tokens — into their international remittance infrastructure.

initiative: Leading Iranian crypto exchange Nobitex was founded by sons of elite political family tied to supreme leaders: Reuters
description: A Reuters investigation has identified the founders of Nobitex — Iran's dominant crypto exchange handling roughly 70% of the country's crypto activity and claiming 11 million users — as Ali and Mohammad Kharrazi, members of a family connected by marriage to all three of Iran's supreme leaders, who registered the platform under an alternative surname in corporate and marketing filings.
